speaker: interviewer
text: When he got to Chust with his brother.

speaker: interviewee
text: We got to Chust, and we stayed there overnight. The people wanted to give us a bed, but we didn't even want to get into a bed. We knew we were very filthy. But my father had a business associate by the name of David Schwartz-- that was a wealthy family that lived in-- this family that he dropped us off was a poor family. The house, I don't I don't even think that they had inside, inside plumbing in that house. It was at the edge of the city. We wanted to get to this family, David Schwartz. Next, next day during, during, during the day we were able to, to get over there to this family, Da-- David Schwartz. They, they took us in, very, very, very-- we were very happy and surprised. We still didn't know why this Dudie Neuman, what what he said. We didn't know that. We found that out later. And in-- and in this house, the first thing what we did was ask for a bath, but-- because in four months time-- I don't remember exactly the date where it was, but it was a good four months that we didn't have a bath. So we stayed in that house. And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] the next day-- I, I think either the next or the th-- second day [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] after, after we, we, we bathed, and we were-- I, I fell asleep from exhaustion. I was-- I slept 12 hours, and then I slept-- all of sudden I remember I, I wake up, and I hear the voice of my father. My father came from Mlawa to Chust, and he knew where we are going to be.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I as-- and when I woke up, I see him sitting at the table talking to my older brother, Tsvi, and asking, ask-- asking questions about what happened. Where is Moshe, you know, the rest of the family? [PAUSES FOR 8 SECONDS] I jumped out of bed.

speaker: interviewee
text: [CRYING] I noticed right away that my father aged in those four months. [CRYING] Anyway, it was a happy, happy reunion. And not too far from Chust-- I told-- I told the story before, that we sent some stuff away to my father's sister that lived close by-- only twenty kilometers from the city of Chust. And in among those things, I had-- my clothing were there.

speaker: interviewee
text: So he said he is going to go travel back to Berezovo-- was the name of the place he traveled back. And he came with his sister. They came the next day again. This was-- this took another day. We were already at, at that time in this David Schwartz's house. And he brought us some fresh clothing for, for both of us, for Tsvi and for myself.

speaker: interviewee
text: And also we asked him to bring-- to bring a, a, a shaver-- a shaver and a-- and a-- and a hair cutter because we were filthy, filthy with lice after so many months. [SIGHS] And of course, when he came the next day with his sister, they brought-- they brought fresh clothing. And, and the-- his sister started asking about the rest of the family. And we, we just told them simply that we don't know. Of course, we knew that so-- that what was going on at Kamenets-Podol'skii, that they, they, they, they, they are not alive anymore. Then I, I remember my father decided-- we had two uncles that lived-- the deeper you get into Hungary, the safer you were because, because this area was governed by the military because it was close to the military zone. And even the civilian government didn't have com-- complete con-- control over every, every general that was in this area recommended-- he was the, the, the guard. He did whatever he wanted to do.

speaker: interviewee
text: So it was better to get into deeper into Hungary. So my father hired a taxi. He didn't want us to travel by train or, or, or to the city of Sa-- [? Sakmir ?] I mar-- marked it at-- the Romanians called it Satu Mare. The Hungarians called it Szatmarnemeti.

speaker: interviewee
text: We had two uncles. My bro-- my mother-- my mother's brother lived there. And my father's sister lived there. My father's sister's husband, his name was Altar Fried. He had a soda factory in, in the city of Sakmir. And my brother, my Uncle Darvas Armin, which was my mother's younger, younger brother, he was a retired schoolteacher. And his wife was a re-- re-- they lived in, in, in that city. Also my mother's a cousin owned a hotel in Satu Mare, in this city.

speaker: interviewee
text: So it was-- it was decided that my father hired a taxi. I remember he paid $300 for the taxi to take us to Satu Mare. It was safe-- safer to get out. And, and we should get into that hotel. The hotel's name was Pannonia. And over there, we should get in touch with, with my mother's cousin. And that's how, how it happened.

speaker: interviewee
text: We got-- we got into-- we, of course, got already cleaned up. We had different clothing. We shaved. And we came to Satu Mare and to that hotel. And we asked for the-- they were the owners of that hotel. When we got over there, we found out that her father, which was my mother's uncle, was one of the 17, 19 people that was left in Torun, that he left Torun. That he-- his daughter that owned the hotel took him away and told him, you have nothing to, to, to stay there anymore. And he stayed with her in the hotel. They gave him a room in the hotel there. And when we got in there, they put us into this room where, where this Uncle [INAUDIBLE] was. And of course, he started asking about his granddaughter and his family. And the same thing again, we, we, we, we told him, we don't know. But anyway, he was able to contact-- now I don't know how they contacted-- the two uncles-- and he was able to contact them I think with a messenger because I don't think they have telephones-- to come over. When they came over, they were very surprised to see us, see us over there. And this grand uncle of ours, he said, here are Abraham [INAUDIBLE] two children. You are two uncles. You divide among ourselves. You take one, and you take one. And of course, they, they agreed and it felt-- My older brother Tsvi kind of felt that I would-- I would feel-- I would feel more comfortable to stay with my mother's brother, with the Darvas family because they were more modern and, and not so [NON-ENGLISH]. And the other were [NON-ENGLISH]. And he knew that I wouldn't feel too well anyway. He sized up the situation pretty well. And so we got divided up. My brother Tsvi went to my brother Altar and my-- and I went to brother Darvas. Darvas, his wife was very sick at that time. They had five children, two sons, three daughters, all college, college graduates. And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] they didn't speak Yiddish because they spoke Hungarian in the house and Romanian. And I didn't know Romanian. So I came into the house. The only one I could speak was with my uncle. My aunt knew a little bit German. She understood Yiddish a little bit. But she wasn't too long in that house because she, she was sent to the city of Debrecen because she was very sick, and she died a half a year later. Here are we are in, in Satu Mare again. And in Satu Mare, I stayed-- this was-- this was the end of October. I stayed November, December, and January. Three months I stayed with my Uncle Darvas hiding out in that-- in that-- in his house. He was a retired teacher. He used to go out. His wife [INAUDIBLE] who was in the hospital. She died later. The younger daughter was in college. The, the older daughter was married and away somewhere. And there was another one that was home. And, and the only way I was able to communicate was I had to-- I had to talk in Hungarian, which I picked up very fast. If you don't-- if you have no choice, you learn it fast. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I suffered terribly from hunger in that house because they were retired teachers, and they were living on a pension. And in the city at that time the, the people were getting rations. Bread was rationed. Fat was rationed. Milk was rationed, everything. And of course I was illegal, so they had to share. And those rations weren't-- weren't too, too generous. So they had to share it with me. I used to see-- the house was ran by one of the daughters. When the mother was away, it was ran like a factory-- around the clock, the table-- there was was plenty of order on the table with white napkins and white tablecloth. But when I looked at the food that was put up on the table, I used to think to myself, maybe that would be enough for me, maybe.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I decided, in that case, I won't even-- I, I won't even finish what I'm-- what I'm getting. Because that's how de-- not dehydrated-- I was so hungry from those four, five months not having a decent meal. So it, it was terrible. But this, I talk about it because it was important to me at that time. But I mana-- I managed to, to overcome-- to overcome that.

speaker: interviewee
text: The same thing happened to my brother the-- in the other place-- not as bad. Fortunately, in deep side in Hungary, in a city of Mariapocs, there was a cousin of ours. His name, Sanyi Fixler-- he passed away a few years ago. He was-- passed away in Miami Beach. He had a mom and pop store in that city. It's-- it was-- it wasn't a city. It was a village. And the area where that Mariapocs is is called [? Nyiregyhaza ?] in Hungarian. And this is the bras-- breadbasket of, of Hungary. From that area, they supplied bread for the entire German army during the wartime.

speaker: interviewee
text: So but the people over there, they had no rationing. So when he found out what was going on, he came, and he took my brother Tsvi over there. And he was very close with the local authorities. So that he told them that he needs a teacher-- a Hebrew teacher-- and he almost make my brother legal there. As long as he stayed there, he was, he was OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: As far as I'm concerned, I, I, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I don't know what happened. Either I had another-- my, my-- I should have said in the beginning, I come from a very large family. My father had eight sisters and a brother. There were ten in the family and ten on my mother's side. So and they were scattered all over Hungary, Romania, Czechoslovakia and those three because under Austria-Hungary, this was all one.

speaker: interviewee
text: So one, one of his sisters lived in Bihor Diosig. I don't know if my, my father contacted them, or they did it on their own. One day two of the-- my cousins, two girls, came, and they said to me, you are hungr-- hungry over here. You are going to come to stay with us. And, and, and they took me. Already by then I was able-- I was able to go to the train station and buy a, a train ticket. I knew enough of the language. So it wa-- it wasn't-- it wasn't suspicious because usually if you bought for somebody a train ticket, that was already suspicious. During the wartime, they were watching, watching these things very carefully. How much time do we have?

speaker: interviewee
text: OK, we managed-- we managed to get to, to-- I managed to get to Bihor Diosig, where my Uncle [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Goldberg lived. He was in the wine business. And also in that area there was no rationing or really anything. There was plenty of food in the house. And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I stayed there-- I stayed there-- be-- before-- before I, I, I, I have to backtrack a little bit. I have to tell this story. In December-- in December, I was still in, in, in Satu Mare-- yeah, in Satu Mare. My cousin [INAUDIBLE] was alone, and she said-- she said to me, I want to go to the movies. Come with me. I was very afraid to go out. She says, don't worry. Let's-- we'll just-- we'll just go as a couple. We'll-- under arm-- you take me under your arm. And we'll go to the movies. And we went to the movies. And we saw an Amer-- it was an American movie. I think-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] 'Somewhere Over the Rainbow,' I remember that, that song, Judy-- Judy Garland. Judy Garland Yeah. Anyway why am I backtracking? We got out from the movies, and on the corners of the-- of the streets, usually you had newspaper boys screaming out the latest-- the latest headlines. And a newspaper boy-- there was a newspaper called [NON-ENGLISH] He's screaming out, the United States was attacked by the-- by the, the imperial country of Japan. And the Axis battle in Rome is now battle in Rome, Tokyo and-- And when I saw that-- so my cousin went over, and she bought a paper because I wanted to know what was-- and she told me this was December the 7th when Pearl Harbor was, was attacked. And it was the happiest day in my hiding, I must admit, because I knew that the United States is going into the war. And definitely the [NON-ENGLISH] Hitler will have a bloody demise. That's why I went back to December. But in January sometimes, we went-- I left Debrecen-- I mean Satu Mare, Satmar, and I came to Bihor Diosig with my-- with my family, the Goldbergs. And again, we had to go through the same thing. You know, a sister, she was asking about her brothers and sisters. And, and of course, I, I, I, I told my, my old story that I don-- I don't know. I don't know what happened to them.

speaker: interviewee
text: So here we are in Bihor Diosig. From there, we stayed-- we stayed there till-- I stayed there till May sometimes. I was like in a-- in a house arrest. They had a house that was in the-- on the main street. And they were in business. And I, I knew that when people come in, I shouldn't be in the living room. I was-- I was in the-- in the-- like in house arrest. But around May time, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] somebody-- this uncle of mine had a-- had a-- OK-- had a business dispute with, with somebody. And I snuck in one Friday night-- on the same street, there was a synagogue. And I snuck in Friday night into the synagogue. And this, this antagonist of my uncle, he saw a strange face, and he kind of inquired, who is that-- who is that stranger there?

speaker: interviewee
text: And they started being afraid that he is going to, to say something. And I started feeling this-- listening that there-- there is whisperings in the house. And so I decided it's, it's time to get out. It's not safe anymore here. And this-- this was sometimes around May 1942.

speaker: interviewee
text: And by then, I found out-- by then, I found out that my father was able to send, in January, a, a messenger to [INAUDIBLE] for my brother Moshe. Can you imagine how much effort they put in, in, in, in money to save their children? And they were lucky to bring him over. And the same-- the same detective that they hired to ferry us, he was the one that brought him over. And they send him also into Satmar. So by February, we were already lucky that the entire three brothers in the families are back safely in, into Hungary.

speaker: interviewee
text: But what happened a few weeks later was among the 15 or 17 people that were left, four or five were found dead by their houses. So some of the soldiers that were there thought that they-- they have money. They murdered them to get their money. So my parents, once this happened, they left-- they left their own house in Torun. And they went away to, to Mlawa, to there. Because over there, he also had a sister living there that was left. So, so they lost their own-- their own house too. But they were long enough home to be able to help us to came acro-- to come across, and to help my other brother to come across. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I found-- I found that out in, in May. My father would correspond with my brother Eliezer in Debrecen. With us, he would-- he wouldn't correspond because he was afraid. So whatever, whatever messages we did get, we would get by code from, from my brother, Eliezer. And, and as I told you, when he escaped that fateful [NON-ENGLISH] night, August the 2nd, that night, a few nights later, he managed to get back to Hungary and unite with his wife and children. And he lived in Debrecen.

speaker: interviewee
text: So when I got scared what happened in Diosig by my Uncle Goldberg, where am I going to go? So I left for Debrecen to go to my brother. That was the first time I left. And, and I came to Debrecen. I think we're just about--
